prompt: What ML methods are used to control and optimize metal additive manufacturing by monitoring in-situ thermal and mechanical measurements? I need to understand what handles the sequential/temporal nature of thermal history data, and what the current gaps are between simulation-based and hardware-validated approaches.

assertions.text: Output explicitly characterizes the data regime for AM (continuous temporal stream from in-situ sensors, limited labeled examples, expensive evaluations per print)
Output flags that the sequential/temporal nature of thermal history data violates the i.i.d. assumption of standard feedforward models, and names which method families handle this
Sim-to-real gap is named with at least one specific physics the simulation omits (e.g., melt pool dynamics, microstructure evolution, residual stress)
An engineering baseline is identified — what a process engineer would use without ML (e.g., PID control, design of experiments, empirical process maps)
References use numbered format [1], [2] inline with a numbered list at the end
Method selection for at least one family is explicitly justified from problem characteristics (data regime, uncertainty need, or physics structure) — not just described
